metadata.title: The Brand Consistency Audit
metadata.playlist: Build a Home — Thought Leadership on Substack
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 10 brief — Self-Promotion Through Thought Leadership

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 2 of 5. Part 1 of the assignment has two graded pieces: the Substack setup from Episode 1 and a brand consistency audit. The audit is ten points and it is not difficult — it is a screenshot comparison of three surfaces with a short written explanation of how they cohere. But the shortcut that costs those ten points is submitting three surfaces that do not match and writing 'they are cohesive' with no evidence. This episode is fast. Let's go.

props.command: 3 screenshots side by side (Substack / LinkedIn / website) + 3–5 sentence explanation of how they cohere.
props.runningText: 'it's cohesive' with no evidence = 0 points; named elements + screenshots = 10 points…
output: Screenshots: Substack header, LinkedIn header, website hero — side by side
3–5 sentence explanation naming palette, type, logo, voice from A7–8

beats.narration_text: The three surfaces. First: the Substack header — take a screenshot of the top of your Substack publication page as it appears to a visitor who is not logged in. This shows the publication name, the tagline, and the header image. Second: the LinkedIn header — take a screenshot of your LinkedIn profile at the top, showing the banner image and profile photo. Third: the website hero — the top of your portfolio website from Assignment 8. If you do not have a website, use the top of your portfolio or the main page from A8. Take all three screenshots at the same zoom level and the same viewport width. When they are placed side by side, a viewer should be able to look at all three and say 'these are clearly from the same person or organization' — even if the content is different. The elements that create that recognition are shared palette, shared type treatment, a logo that appears on at least two of the three surfaces, and a consistent voice in any text that is visible. Surfaces that do not match — different dominant colors, unrelated fonts, a logo that appears on one but not the others — signal a brand that was built in three separate sessions without coordination.

props.artifactHeading: Substack header · LinkedIn header · Website hero
artifactLines: Substack: top of publication page as a non-logged-in visitor sees it.
LinkedIn: top of profile — banner + profile photo.
Same zoom level and viewport width for all 3.
Side-by-side test: a viewer says 'clearly same person/brand' without reading the name.
props.sparkLine: 3 surfaces. Same zoom. Same width. A viewer should say 'same person' without reading.

beats.narration_text: Three to five sentences explaining how the three surfaces cohere. This is not 'they all use my brand colors' — it is naming the specific elements. The explanation has three parts. First: what is shared. Name the primary color or palette, the font or type treatment, and the logo or mark. 'All three surfaces use the same navy and cream palette from the Assignment 7 style guide. The sans-serif heading font appears on the Substack header and the website hero. The logo mark from Assignment 7 appears on the LinkedIn banner and the Substack header.' Second: how they are distinct without breaking coherence. 'The LinkedIn banner uses a photography element not present on the Substack — but it is the same color as the Substack header's background.' Not identical means each surface is optimized for its context. Identical would be lazy; coherent is intentional. Third: what the voice coherence looks like. If the tagline on the Substack, the LinkedIn headline, and the portfolio bio all describe the same person in consistent language — specific and direct — that is voice coherence. One sentence on that. Assemble the three screenshots and the explanation as a one-page PDF. Put the screenshots at the top, the explanation below them.

props.artifactHeading: Name the elements · note the distinctions · one line on voice
artifactLines: Part 1 — Shared elements: name the palette + type treatment + logo placement on each surface.
Part 2 — Intentional distinctions: how each surface adapts to its context without breaking the brand.
Part 3 — Voice coherence: tagline + LinkedIn headline + portfolio bio describe the same person in consistent language.
'They are cohesive' = not evidence. Named elements with examples = evidence.
One-page PDF: screenshots at top, explanation below.
props.sparkLine: Named elements with examples. Not assertions.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for the Brand Consistency Audit. Three screenshots at the same zoom and viewport: Substack header, LinkedIn header, website hero. Side by side, a viewer should recognize they are from the same person or brand without reading the name. Three to five sentences naming the shared elements — palette, type, logo placement — how each surface adapts to its context without breaking coherence, and one line on voice coherence. Not 'they are cohesive' — named elements with examples. Assembled as a one-page PDF: screenshots at the top, explanation below. Episode 3 writes the thought-leadership article.

artifactLines: 3 screenshots (Substack / LinkedIn / website) — same zoom, same viewport width.
Side-by-side test: viewer recognizes same brand without reading the name.
Explanation: named palette + type + logo · intentional distinctions · voice coherence.
Not 'they are cohesive.' Named elements with examples.
One-page PDF: screenshots top, explanation below.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. After you take the three screenshots, use this prompt to draft the coherence explanation. Paste it into Claude with your own answers. The prompt reads: I am writing a brand consistency audit for Assignment 10. I have screenshots of three brand surfaces. Here is what each surface uses: Substack header — primary color: [name or hex]. Type treatment: [font name or description]. Logo or mark present: yes or no; if yes, describe its placement. LinkedIn banner — primary color: [name or hex]. Type treatment: [font name or description]. Logo or mark present: yes or no. Website hero — primary color: [name or hex]. Type treatment: [font or description]. Logo or mark present: yes or no. Intentional differences across the three: [describe one to two ways the surfaces adapt to their context — for example, photography on LinkedIn not on Substack]. Voice across the three: my tagline is [paste it], my LinkedIn headline is [paste it], my portfolio bio opener is [paste it]. Write a three-to-five sentence coherence explanation that: names the specific shared elements with one example per element, describes one intentional distinction and why it does not break coherence, and adds one sentence on whether the voice in the tagline, headline, and bio reads as the same person. Do not write 'they are cohesive' without evidence.

props.command: 3 surfaces — per surface: primary color · type treatment · logo (yes/no + placement). Intentional differences: [describe]. Voice: tagline [paste] + LinkedIn headline [paste] + bio opener [paste]. Write 3–5 sentences: named shared elements + 1 intentional distinction (coherence preserved) + 1 line on voice.
props.runningText: named elements with examples — not assertions; the grader checks the screenshots against the explanation…

beats.narration_text: The Brand Consistency Audit. Liam, in for Bear.
